description: The provided image appears to be a placeholder icon, rendering the visual content unreadable. Based on the paper context describing warm deformation of Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low density steel, this figure likely presents true stress-strain curves obtained from compression tests conducted at 500°C and 600°C to true strains of 0.3 and 0.6 at a strain rate of 0.001 s⁻¹. The text reports flow softening after strain 0.3 at 500°C attributed to bidirectional deformation-induced transformations (DIAT and DIFT), while tests at 600°C show higher stress levels without pronounced softening due to better strain distribution. Alternatively, if this represents microstructural characterization, it may depict EBSD phase maps or TEM images showing dislocation cells, high dislocation density walls, and phase transformations along deformation bands, contrasting the banded structure formed at 500°C with the uniform deformation at 600°C.

Figure 1 illustrates the initial microstructure and compressive deformation behavior of a triplex Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel annealed at 950°C for 1 hour. Subfigure (a) presents an EBSD phase map utilizing band slope contrast to differentiate ferrite (green), austenite (red), and thermally-induced martensite (yellow). Subfigure (b) displays a TEM bright-field micrograph revealing the morphological arrangement of austenite (γ), ferrite (α), and martensite (α'), accompanied by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns indexed to confirm the BCC crystal structure of both ferrite and martensite and the FCC structure of austenite; white arrows indicate initial ferrite-ferrite boundaries. Subfigures (c) and (d) characterize the mechanical response: (c) shows true stress-strain curves obtained at 500°C and 600°C under a strain rate of 0.001 s⁻¹, exhibiting flow softening beyond true strain 0.3 at 500°C, while (d) plots the temperature dependence (100–700°C) of maximum flow stress and yield strength (0.2% offset), highlighting mechanical stability from 200–500°C and a pronounced stress drop between 500°C and 600°C (marked by a blue dashed oval). These data collectively establish the starting triplex microstructure and the temperature-dependent mechanical stability that underpins subsequent strain-induced phase transformations.

This figure presents EBSD phase maps overlaid on image quality (IQ) maps and corresponding phase distribution maps of Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel after warm compression. Subfigures (a) and (c) show the microstructure deformed at 500°C under a strain rate of 0.001 s⁻¹ to a true strain of 0.6, revealing frequently distributed deformation bands within the BCC ferrite matrix (green) containing FCC austenite (red). Subfigures (b) and (d) display the sample compressed at 600°C under identical strain conditions, exhibiting a uniformly deformed microstructure without prominent banding. The detailed phase maps (c) and (d) distinguish three constituent phases: ferrite (green), austenite (red), and martensite (yellow). The comparison demonstrates that deformation at 500°C induces bi-directional ferrite↔austenite transformation along strain-localized bands, driven by Mn partitioning that equalizes Gibbs free energy between phases, whereas the higher temperature of 600°C enables homogeneous strain distribution suppressing such transformation. These observations support the mechanism of deformation-induced phase transformation governing the flow softening behavior.

Figure 3 presents TEM bright-field micrographs, SAED patterns, and phase maps of Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel subjected to warm compression. Subfigure (a) shows the dislocation configuration within ferrite at 500°C and interrupted strain of 0.3, revealing tangled dislocations forming cell structures, high dislocation density walls (HDDWs), and primary/secondary slip traces on {110} and {112} planes. Subfigure (b) displays the formation of fresh ferrite grains (indicated by white arrows) within parent austenite islands under identical conditions, accompanied by SAED patterns confirming the BCC ferrite (Z=[113]) and FCC austenite (Z=[-111]) structures. Subfigures (c) and (d) provide higher magnification phase maps at strain 0.6 and 500°C, illustrating austenite shearing along deformation bands; red and green arrows denote austenite and ferrite formation through deformation-induced austenite transformation (DIAT) and deformation-induced ferrite transformation (DIFT), respectively. Subfigure (e) shows the microstructure at 600°C, where caption states no deformation bands or transformation features are observed. Collectively, these images demonstrate simultaneous bidirectional ferrite-austenite transformations as a strain compensation mechanism at 500°C, which is absent at higher temperatures.

This figure presents Thermo-Calc® thermodynamic modeling results for Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel. Subfigures (a) and (b) show equilibrium mass percentages of Mn and Al partitioning among BCC_A2 (ferrite), FCC_A1 (austenite), and FCC_A1#2 phases as functions of temperature, with vertical lines marking the annealed temperature (~950°C). Subfigure (a) reveals Mn strongly partitions into austenite (decreasing from ~75% to ~12% as temperature rises) versus ferrite (increasing to ~10%), while (b) shows Al preferentially partitions into ferrite (~6%) over austenite (~5%). Subfigures (c-f) display Gibbs free energy curves for BCC and FCC phases at 500°C and 550°C as functions of Mn% (c, e) and Al% (d, f). Point "A" marks the composition where both phases possess equal Gibbs free energy. Points "B" (FCC) and "C" (BCC) indicate actual chemical compositions derived from elemental partitioning data (Table 1: ferrite ~10.3% Mn/6.0% Al, austenite ~12.8% Mn/4.7% Al). The diagrams demonstrate that Mn partitioning primarily dictates phase stability, bringing both phases to nearly identical Gibbs free energy at 500°C (dashed line intersection in c), whereas varying Al content has negligible influence (d, f). This thermodynamic equality provides the driving force for simultaneous deformation-induced ferrite-to-austenite and austenite-to-ferrite transformations observed during warm deformation.
